coredata.dc:title: Methotrexate-loaded manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots as targeted nano drug-delivery system for potential use in cancer theranostics
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Singh, Harish
Dhar, Dhruba
Das, Soumen
Halder, Mintu
coredata.dc:description: The present work highlights manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots as a novel pH-sensitive drug delivery system, for the delivery of methotrexate, targeted to cancer cells, along with the simultaneous real-time monitoring of cellular uptake and drug delivery using fluorescence imaging. Briefly, methotrexate was conjugated with the as-prepared quantum dots to form a nano drug-delivery system through non-covalent interactions. The developed material demonstrated drug loading efficiency of ∼91 % and a very efficient (∼80 %) pH-responsive drug release at pH 5.5. The cytotoxic properties of the developed nano-platform viz. manganese, nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots, and methotrexate conjugated manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot complex were evaluated in cancer cells (HeLa) and healthy human cells (L929). The conjugated complex demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, high cellular uptake, and a substantial drug release. Healthy human cells were largely unaffected when incubated with both the MTX-conjugated Mn-, N-CQD and the free drug. Additionally, in vitro, the fluorescence bio-imaging potential of the developed nano-platform was also assessed, where the unconjugated (free) quantum dots exhibited better blue fluorescence imaging potential compared to that of the conjugated complex, which can be attributed to fluorescence quenching due to the presence of methotrexate. This study highlights the manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots capable of pH-responsive methotrexate drug delivery selective to cancer cells. The combination of drug delivery and fluorescence imaging creates an efficient platform for targeted therapy, allowing very precise drug release while accessing non-invasive monitoring of therapeutic response. This method holds a great promise for advancing cancer treatment strategies, with the potential for increased efficacy and decreased side effects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence
Folate
Methotrexate
pH-sensitive drug delivery
Cancer theranostics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells
Characterisation of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex
The ionic strength and pH response of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex
In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M. H. thanks to IIT Kharagpur for financial supports. HS thanks DST-INSPIRE for his fellowship. We thank Prof. N. Sarkar and Prof. J. Chatterjee for performing DLS experiment and MTT assay in their lab, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), Size distribution (b), and hydrodynamic size distribution (c) of Mn-, N-CQD.
TEM image (a), Size distribution (b), and hydrodynamic size distribution (c) of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex.
Overlayed FTIR spectra of Mn-, N-CQD, MTX and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex (a) and XRD spectra of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex (b).
Full range X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy spectra of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex.

Overlayed absorption and emission spectra of Mn-, N-CQD (a), absorption spectra of Mn-, N-CQD, MTX and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex (b), normalised absorption spectra of the complex at different concentrations of MTX (c), and emission spectra (after correction for quencher absorption at the excitation wavelength) of Mn-, N-CQD in presence of various concentrations of MTX (d) (Emission intensities in a. u.).
Methotrexate release efficiency (%) of Mn-, N-CQD as a function of time.
Analysis of in vitro cytotoxicity (MTT assay) of Mn-, N-CQD (between 0–60 μg/mL concentrations) on HeLa cells (a), and L929 cells (b) at 24 and 48 h.

Assessment of in vitro anticancer efficacy of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (at 0–80 μM MTX concentrations) compared to free MTX on HeLa cells [(a) at 24 h, (b) at 48 h], L929 fibroblasts [(c) at 24 h, (d) at 48 h].

Live /Dead staining of HeLa cells (a-l) and L929 cells (m-x) due to incubation with Mn-, N-CQD (at 20 µg mL−1) (images: d, e, f, p, q, and r); free MTX (50 µM) (images g, h, i, s, t and u); and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (MTX: 50 µM) (images j, k, l, v, w, and x) for 48 h.

Brightfield images (a and c) of HeLa cells; and the corresponding fluorescent images of HeLa cells: Fluorescence image after 1-hour incubation with Mn-, N-CQD (b), Fluorescence image after 1-hour incubation with Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (d).

a) Fluorescence images (a), (c), (e), and (g)) and corresponding bright field images ((b), (d), (f,) and (h)) of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (MTX: 50 µM) incubated HeLa cells. ((a) and (c)) Fluorescence image of cells under a neutral condition (pH: 7.4) after 24 h (a) and 48 h (c) incubation with Mn-, N-CQD/MTX. ((e) and (g)) Fluorescence image of cells under an acidic condition (pH: 5.5) after 24 h (e) and 48 h (g) incubation with Mn-, N-CQD/MTX.
Synthesis of Mn- N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex and representation of as-synthesized Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex for cancer diagnosis.

head.ce:title: Methotrexate-loaded manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots as targeted nano drug-delivery system for potential use in cancer theranostics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, Kharagpur 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex as a nano drug delivery system for cancer theranostics.

The present work highlights manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots as a novel pH-sensitive drug delivery system, for the delivery of methotrexate, targeted to cancer cells, along with the simultaneous real-time monitoring of cellular uptake and drug delivery using fluorescence imaging. Briefly, methotrexate was conjugated with the as-prepared quantum dots to form a nano drug-delivery system through non-covalent interactions. The developed material demonstrated drug loading efficiency of ∼91 % and a very efficient (∼80 %) pH-responsive drug release at pH 5.5. The cytotoxic properties of the developed nano-platform viz. manganese, nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots, and methotrexate conjugated manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dot complex were evaluated in cancer cells (HeLa) and healthy human cells (L929). The conjugated complex demonstrated excellent biocompatibility, high cellular uptake, and a substantial drug release. Healthy human cells were largely unaffected when incubated with both the MTX-conjugated Mn-, N-CQD and the free drug. Additionally, in vitro, the fluorescence bio-imaging potential of the developed nano-platform was also assessed, where the unconjugated (free) quantum dots exhibited better blue fluorescence imaging potential compared to that of the conjugated complex, which can be attributed to fluorescence quenching due to the presence of methotrexate. This study highlights the manganese nitrogen dual-doped carbon quantum dots capable of pH-responsive methotrexate drug delivery selective to cancer cells. The combination of drug delivery and fluorescence imaging creates an efficient platform for targeted therapy, allowing very precise drug release while accessing non-invasive monitoring of therapeutic response. This method holds a great promise for advancing cancer treatment strategies, with the potential for increased efficacy and decreased side effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a class of diseases caused by uncontrolled cell growth, and these abnormal cells are able to spread or invade other parts of the body, ultimately leading to death. The World Health Organisation reported that cancer caused nearly 10 million deaths in 2020, which represents almost one in the six deaths worldwide due to the cancer [1]. Most common cancer treatment is to use chemotherapeutic drugs, including fluorouracil (FU), paclitaxel (PTX), doxorubicin (DOX), and methotrexate (MTX) to kill the fast-growing cancer cells. In the conventional approaches to cancer treatment, chemotherapeutic drugs not only attack on neoplastic cells but also induce severe side effects on healthy cells and tissues [2–4]. Additionally, there are other drawbacks, such as lack of cell permeation, bioavailability, and efficiency of these drugs [5]. Therefore, prime focus is on how to kill or attenuate cancer cells, keeping normal cells mostly unaffected. To address this critical issue, drug delivery systems are being attempted for their superior efficiency in the loading and releasing of molecules and targeting cancer cells [6–8]. Recently, several carriers have been introduced as alternatives for cancer treatment, including magnetic carriers [9], polymers-based nano-formulations [10], extracellular vehicles [11], polysaccharides [12], and mesoporous silica, but metal- or carbon-based nano drug-delivery methods [13–20] have gained much interest in the ongoing investigations to reduce the adverse effects caused by the classical approaches.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have good quantum yields, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity and have the potential to become biomarkers and drug carriers [21–24]. The hydrophilic surface functional groups of carbon quantum dots may enhance the receptor binding affinity through covalent or non-covalent interactions when combined with chemotherapeutic drugs [22–27]. To accomplish selectivity against cancer cells, these nanocarriers administer the gradual release of the drug while increasing the targeted drug’s permeability to cancer cells [7,28]. MTX has a very similar structure to folic acid, which is also known as a folate antagonist drug (Fig. S1). The content of folate receptors is higher in HeLa cells than the normal human fibroblast cells [29]; therefore, MTX is expected to be capable of entering cells via a similar transportation system of folic acid [30,31]. Compared to folic acid, MTX has a substantially stronger affinity for the folic acid receptors. A recognised biomarker of tumour cells is the overexpression of folic acid receptors found in many cancer cells [32]. In order to prevent cancer cells from absorbing folic acid, MTX competes for the folic acid cycle. This leads to the production of purine and pyrimidine, which disrupts DNA/RNA synthesis and ultimately slows down cell development [33].

Most of the Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast sensors are based on gadolinium (Gd III) [34]. However, Gd (III) is known to be nephrotoxic, leading to nephrogenic systemic fibrosis. On that account, the use of Gd (III)-doped carbon dots in patients with renal impairment is limited. Manganese-based nanomaterials are promising alternatives due to their lower toxicity compared to Gd (III) [35]. In addition, there is no preclinical data available regarding the toxicity of Mn-based nanomaterials in vitro and in vivo, which makes them alternative to Gd (III) [36].

Herein, drug delivery of MTX via Mn-, N-CQD as a nanocarrier has been investigated (Scheme 1 ). The electrostatic force was used to conjugate the Mn-, N-CQD carrier to MTX by taking advantage of its strong affinity for the folate receptors. The morphology of Mn-, N-CQD, and methotrexate-conjugate carbon quantum dot (Mn-, N-CQD/MTX) complex has been investigated by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD). The information on surface functionalization and charge was obtained from the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Zeta potential measurements, respectively. The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of the uncomplexed (bare) Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex are evaluated in vitro for the human cervical cancer cell line and human fibroblast cell line. The morphological changes in cells were analysed by using confocal fluorescence microscopy.

PEI, Citric acid trisodium salt (SC, 98 %), Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O), Whatman Grade 41 filter paper, and Dialysis bag (MWCO −3500 Dalton) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Calcein-AM (C1430) and propidium iodide (P3566) dyes were procured from Thermo Fisher Scientific. A phosphate buffer solution (10 mM) was used in this experiment. Triple distilled water was used to prepare the different aqueous solutions. Other reagents were analytical reagent grade and used without any further purification.

The observations with analytical TEM were made at 200 kV. The size distribution and charge of the nanoparticles were determined by using the Malvern Nano Zeta sizer instrument. The absorption spectra were recorded using the Shimadzu UV-2600 absorption spectrophotometer, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded using Flurolog-3. The Nikon Eclipse TS2R fluorescence microscope was used for all the fluorescence microscopic studies. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum was recorded by using a PHI-5000 Versa probe II spectrometer with an Al Kα X-Ray monochromator (1486.7 eV) by drop-casting a freshly prepared sample on the glass plate. The binding energy scale was calibrated by the standard value of C 1s at 284.6 eV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) measurements were made using a Perkin Elmer FTIR spectrophotometer. The proton nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded using 500 MHz of Bruker ultrashield TM 500.

The Mn-, N-CQD was prepared by hydrothermal treatment, according to a previously reported procedure [37]. Briefly, 0.1 M sodium citrate, 0.1 M manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate, and 50 μM polyethyleneimine (PEI) were dissolved in 10 mL of triple distilled water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then, the solution was heated at 160 °C for 8 h (Scheme 1). The resulting solution was filtered by using a Whatman filter paper of grade 41 to discard large-sized nanoparticles, and further, the filtrate solution was dialysed in a dialysis bag for 2 h with a molecular weight cuff off 3500 Dalton to remove unreacted chemicals. The Mn-, N-CQD solution was kept at 4 °C in the dark.

The stock solution (11 mg mL−1) of Mn-, N-CQD was prepared in pH 7.4 phosphate buffer, whereas 40 mM of methotrexate was prepared in DMSO due to its insolubility in water. Then 2.20 mM methotrexate and 1 mL of Mn-, N-CQD (11 mg mL−1) were mixed with 2 mL phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) to get 3 mL reaction mixture. This solution was then rota-mixed at 300 rpm in dark conditions for 12 h at room temperature to allow sufficient binding between MTX and Mn-, N-CQD.

Photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQY) were estimated by using quinine sulfate (0.1 M in H2SO4) as a standard material. The PL intensity and the absorbance value were measured at 360 nm. QY values were obtained according to the following equation (1):
(1)
Q
Y
CQDs
= Q
Y R
A R
/
A
CQD
I
CQD
/
I R
Whereas ‘A’ is the absorbance value, and ‘I’ is the integrated PL intensity. The subscript “R” refers to the standards (QYR = 0.54), and “CQDs” refers to the Mn-, N-CQDs in the present experiment.

These studies were performed under dark conditions. For calculation of drug-loading, the resultant Mn-, N-CQD/MTX solution (3 mL) was dialysed at room temperature by using a dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff 3,500 Dalton) against 75 mL of triple distilled water under continuous stirring for 24 h. The absorbance at 303 nm increases with time and, after 24 h it does not show any change, indicating that the unreacted MTX comes outside the dialysis bag, while the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex remains inside the dialysis bag. Equation (2) was used to estimate the percentage loading capacity and the dialysate absorption was recorded at 303 nm. The pH-dependent MTX release in vitro was carried out at two different pH levels, namely, pH 7.4 and pH 5.5. Briefly, 3 mL of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX solution was dialysed against 75 mL of phosphate buffer solutions in both simulated tumour tissue (41˚C, pH 5.5) and normal physiological conditions (37 ˚C, pH 7.4). To estimate the releasing efficiency, the absorption data of dialysate are monitored in certain time intervals. The percentage of MTX released was evaluated using the equation (3).
(2)
D r u g - l o a d i n g
e f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
( T o t a l
a m o u n t
o f
M T X - U n r e a c t e d
M T X )
To t a l
a m o u n t

M T X
×
100
(3)
Drug - r e l e a s i n g
( % )
=
A m o u n t
o f
r e l e a s e d
M T X
( T o t a l
a m o u n t

M T X - U n r e a c t e d
M T X )
×
100

The in vitro cytotoxicity of the developed Mn-, N-CQD, its drug-conjugated counterpart (Mn-, N-CQD/MTX), along with the free MTX was assessed using MTT assay on HeLa and L929 fibroblasts cells. Briefly, HeLa and L929 cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin antibiotics and maintained under a humidified atmosphere (5 % CO2) at 37 ˚C. Once the cells attained 75–80 % confluence, then they were re-equilibrated with 0.25 % trypsin-0.52 mM EDTA in PBS and collected at a suitable density. This was followed by the seeding of 100 μL of cell suspension at a density of 2 × 104 cells in each well of a 96-well plate. After incubating the cells for 24 h, they were treated with various concentrations of Mn-, N-CQD (0–60 µg mL−1) for 24 h and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (MTX concentration: 0–80 μM, Mn-, N-CQD concentration fixed at 20 µg mL−1) and free drug (0–80 μM) for 48 h (n = 3). Post incubation, the cells were rinsed with 10 mM PBS, and 100 µL of MTT solution (1 mg mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h to form the formazan salts. This was followed by the replacement of the MTT solution with 100 μL of DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals formed in each well. The relative cell viability was computed using equation (4) after measuring absorbance using Multiskan GO instrument (spectrophotometer for photometric assays) at 595 nm.
(4)
Cell
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
OD
o f
c o n t r o l - O D
o f
t r e a t e d
OD
o f
c o n t r o l
× 100

For this study, both the HeLa and L929 cells (8 × 104 cells) were incubated with a certain concentration of Mn-, N-CQD (at 20 µg mL−1), free MTX (50 µM) and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (Mn-, N-CQD: 20 µg mL−1, MTX: 50 µM) for up to 48 h and the samples were stained using 1 × 10−6 M calcein-AM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, C1430) and 2 µg mL−1 propidium iodide (Thermo Fisher Scientific, P3566) to check the cell viabilities. Calcein-AM was used to stain live cells fluorescent green due to their intracellular esterase activity, while the dead cells showed red fluorescence, as propidium iodide could permeate through their compromised membranes and stain their nucleic acids. The fluorescence images are captured using a Nikon Eclipse TS2R fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells
ce:section.ce:para: For the in vitro fluorescence imaging studies, Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQDMTX (MTX: 50 µM) at a concentration of 100 µg mL−1 were added separately to the HeLa cells and incubated for up to 48 h. After incubation, the cells were washed using 10 mM PBS and fluorescence imaging was performed using a Nikon Eclipse TS2R fluorescence microscope at 361–389 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex
ce:section.ce:para: The structure, chemical composition, and morphology of Mn-, N-CQD, and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX were characterized by XRD, XPS, FTIR, TEM and DLS measurements. The TEM images show spherical particles as shown in Fig. 1 (a) and 2 (c), indicating successful formation of Mn-, N-CQD, and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complexes. The size distribution plots showed the average size of Mn-, N-CQD, and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complexes were 3.21 ± 0.05 and 3.83 ± 0.03 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 (b) and 2 (b)). The hydrodynamic size (diameter) was examined via DLS measurements, and the average size (diameter) of Mn-, N-CQD, and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complexes were ∼5.26 ± 0.04 and 6.03 ± 0.12 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 (c) and 2 (c)). The size got slightly bigger due to complex formation, as per both the TEM and DLS data.

The evidence of surface functionalisation was obtained from FTIR measurements. The spectra of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX overlap with some bands of Mn-, N-CQD spectra and some bands of MTX spectra (Fig. 3 (a)). As stretching vibrations of N–H & O-H at 3114–3687 cm−1, [38] the aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations of –CH2CH2 at 2854–2922 cm−1 [39] and the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of COO–, respectively, at 1563 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1 [40] and a small peak at 639 cm−1 assigned to Mn–O indicate that Mn2+ ions have coordinated with CQD [41]. In the spectra of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex, asymmetric and symmetric stretching frequency also exhibited lower IR intensity and a characteristic peak at 644 cm−1 attributed to Mn–O coordination present in the complex. The peaks in the spectra of MTX at 1641 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 are assigned to C=O stretching of the amide group and C-N stretching vibrations, respectively [42,43]. Thus in Mn-, N-CQD/MTX, the peak at 1636 cm−1 and 1437 cm−1 are attributed to C = O stretching of the amide group and C-N stretching, indicating the presence of MTX. Based on the above discussion, we may argue that conjugation of MTX with Mn-, N-CQD due to the non-covalent interactions between the –NH2 group of MTX and –COO– a group of Mn-, N-CQD and –NH2 group of Mn-, N-CQD and –COOH group of MTX. The zeta potential was further assessed to confirm conjugation between Mn-, N-CQD, and MTX. Due to the plentiful presence of carboxylate ions on the surface, the zeta potential for Mn-, N-CQD showed a negative range with an average potential of −14.9 mV. In the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex, the electrostatic contact caused the zeta potential to rise to −9.93 mV. Therefore, the rise in zeta potential indicates that conjugation between the Mn-, N-CQD and MTX has taken place. The proton NMR spectra are also similar to a previous study [37], which shows two singlets at 1.63 ppm and 2.81 ppm for the proton on a carbon adjacent to the amine group and proton on a carbon adjacent to a carboxylic group, respectively (Fig. S2).

Due to the presence of functional groups containing oxygen, the XRD pattern (Fig. 3(b)) of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX shows a broad diffraction peak at ∼24˚, indicating an amorphous nature. For the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex, a comparable XRD pattern was also seen, showing that of the crystal structure of carbon dots is unaffected by the complex formation [44,45].

Further concretization of the structural assessments of Mn-, N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex are done by XPS study and the deconvoluted spectra of each element are depicted in Fig. 4 . The full-range spectra (0–1100 eV) of Mn-, N-CQD, and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex disclose evidence of elemental composition and unmistakably showed the existence of C, O, N, and Mn elements with no other additional peaks (Fig. 4(a)). The integrated area of the associated peak and the atomic sensitivity parameters were used to determine the atomic percentage of C, O, N, and Mn elements. For Mn-, N-CQD, the atomic percentages of C, N, O, and Mn were found at 61.80, 4.24, 26.37, and 7.59 %, respectively. On the other hand, the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex was determined to be 60.80, 4.40, 28.37, and 6.67 %, for C, N, O, and Mn, respectively. Such variation in the percentage of C, N, O, and Mn elements further confirmed the complex formation in the following results of zeta potential and FTIR measurements. The C 1 s spectrum was deconvoluted into four main peaks at 283.2, 284.9, 286.6, and 288.4 eV, which shows that carbon atoms in Mn-, N-CQD, primarily exist as C = C, C–C C-O/C-N and C = O bonding, respectively, indicating that the carbon core contains both sp3 and sp2 carbon orbitals which reflect features of the amorphous nature of C-dots [46,47] (Fig. S3 (a)). Two peaks with binding energies at 529.6 and 531.5 eV, are attributed to C = O and C-OH groups, respectively, and can be seen in the deconvoluted O 1 s spectra (Fig. S3 (c)), indicating the presence of manganese-oxygen bonding and carboxylic functional group [48]. The deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum of Mn-, N-CQD (Fig. S3 (e)), three peaks at 397.7 eV, 399.4 eV, and 401.0 eV, which are related to C–N–C, N–(C)3 and N–H groups, respectively, indicating the presence of amine group [49]. Two strong peaks at 640.8 and 652.6 eV, which correspond to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2, respectively [50,51], show the presence of Mn2+ species in the Mn-, N-CQD (Fig. S3 (g)). Similar to this, Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex underwent XPS spectral analysis to produce the spectra shown in Fig. S3 ((b), (d), (f), and (h)). Compared to the uncomplexed (bare) Mn-N-CQD and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex, the obtained data reveal recognizable peaks for C, N, O, and Mn elements with slight increase in binding energy. The shifting in binding energy may be argued as the interaction of MTX possibly affects the electronic environment of atoms relative to potential bonds present in the Mn-, N-CQD.

The pH 7.4 buffer solution is used for UV–visible absorption and fluorescence spectra of the Mn-, N-CQD, MTX and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex. The n → π* electronic transitions of C = N, C = O and another bond in Mn-, N-CQD are responsible to result in the unique absorption peak at 351 nm. Upon excitation with 360 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission peak was observed at 442 nm with 0.36 ± 0.025 quantum yield (Fig. 5 (a)). Additionally, our previous work [37] has demonstrated the excitation-dependent PL nature of Mn-, N-CQD. The UV–visible spectra of MTX and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex were also recorded and presented in Fig. 5 (b). The observed absorption peaks of the MTX solution at 258, 303 and 372 nm are due to the heteroaromatic pteridine chromophore. The absorption spectrum of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX solution shows three shoulders with high absorbance and blue shift to the free MTX, which can be assigned to be due to the vicinity of MTX and Mn-, N-CQD in the complex resulting in π-π stacking interaction (Fig. 5 (c)). The fluorescence intensity of Mn-, N-CQD decreases by almost 74 % on increasing the concentration from 1 μM to 180 μM of MTX (Fig. 5 (d)). These results demonstrate that MTX and Mn-, N-CQD formed the complex in the solution [52–54], and this presumption is supported by FTIR and XPS spectra, which show high loading of MTX onto the Mn-, N-CQD surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ionic strength and pH response of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of ionic strength of Mn-, N-CQD and different Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complexes was investigated as a function of concentration of sodium chloride (2 mM to 200 mM), and the results confirmi that the salt did not affect on the fluorescence intensity (Figure S4 (a)), giving an impression of the excellent stability in high-salt conditions. To investigate the effect of pH on the binding of MTX with Mn-, N-CQDs, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) was estimated from the slope of the plot between (F0/F)-1 (after correction for absorption due to the quencher) vs concentration of MTX according to equation (5) and listed in Table S1.
(5)
(F
0
/ F)
- 1 =
K
SV
MTX
Where F and F0 denote, respectively, PL intensities of Mn-, N-CQDs in the presence (after correction for absorption due to the quencher) and in the absence of MTX, KSV is the Stern-Volmer (quenching) constant, and [MTX] is the concentration of MTX added in the sample. The Stern-Volmer linear regression equations for the range (5 to 100 μM) are F0/F −1 = 0.00918 [MTX] at pH 3, F0/F −1 = 0.01239[MTX] at pH 5, F0/F −1 = 0.01567 [MTX] at pH 7.4, F0/F −1 = 0.01380[MTX], and F0/F −1 = 0.01396[MTX], respectively. The results show that the value of KSV increases as we move from pH 3 (0.00918 X 106 M−1) to pH 7.4 (0.01567 X 106 M−1), followed by a decreasing trend from pH 7.4 to pH 11 (0. 01396 X 106 M−1). It indicates that the fully anionic form has lower interaction affinity towards Mn-, N-CQD as compared to the form present at pH 7.4. The interaction of MTX and Mn-, N-CQD was affected with pH due to perturbation or alteration in the chemical structure of both MTX and Mn-, N-CQD.

The kinetics of drug-loading was studied by dialysis in triple distilled water for 4 h. At the end of dialysis, dialysate water containing unbound MTX was collected to record absorbance spectra. Based on the observed absorption value of MTX at 303 nm, the loading efficiency was determined to be ∼91 %, indicating the excellent drug-loading ability of Mn-, N-CQD to MTX. The higher value of drug-loading efficiency is due to the several hydrophilic functional groups present in both Mn-, N-CQD and MTX, which facilitate a strong electrostatic interaction between them to form an effective complex.

To study the drug-releasing property at two pH values ​​of 5.5 and 7.4 (Shown in Table S2), and the choice was based on the physiological environments of cancer and normal cells [55]. The drug-releasing efficiency was calculated from observed absorbance and displayed in Fig. 6 . We detected the highest amount of release for MTX (∼80 %) in the cancerous tissue environment (41˚C, pH 5.5). The curve started with burst release from 0 to 12 h and was followed by a slight upward slope to reach maximum release at 48 h. The higher releasing efficiency at pH 5.5 as compared to pH 7.4 (∼60 %) implies that the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex has a selective ability to release the drug in acidic conditions. This unique property of the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex can efficiently facilitate MTX releasing from Mn-, N-CQD in the weak acidic environment of cancer cells and increase the efficacy at low dosages of MTX.

To validate the findings of the MTT assay, live/dead assay was performed using Mn-, N-CQD (at 20 µg mL−1), free MTX (50 µM) and Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (Mn-, N-CQD: 20 µg mL−1, MTX: 50 µM) on HeLa and L929 cell lines (Fig. 9 ) for 48 h. Substantial cell death was observed in HeLa cells, in the case of Mn-, N-CQD/MTX (Fig. 9 (k) and (l)) in comparison to the cells exposed to the same concentration of free MTX, which can be attributed to the sustained release (SR) of the anti-cancer drug along with better cell penetration and internalisation of the drug-delivery platform by the cancer cells. On the contrary, the L929 cells were largely unaffected when incubated with both the MTX-conjugated Mn-, N-CQD and the free drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro fluorescence imaging of cells
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the overexpression in cancer cells, folic acid has a high affinity for folate receptors, which are being used as biomarkers to distinguish tumour cells from normal cells [32,56]. Because cancer cells require significant amounts of folate for growth, low levels of folate receptors are expressed in normal cells compared to a greatly enhanced expression of folate receptors in the majority of tumours [56,57]. As a result, the folate receptor is used as a therapeutic target in the treatment of malignancies as a tumour biomarker.

In this study, we focused on the potential of Mn-, N-CQD as a carrier of MTX for targeted cancer therapy, where we synthesised Mn-, N-CQD emitting bright blue fluorescence and has substantial biocompatibility. This can be further modified to Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex by direct synthesis approach in the aqueous medium. Here, MTX gets loaded to Mn-, N-CQD through non-covalent forces, i.e., weak hydrogen bonding, π- π stacking, and electrostatic interactions. The drug loading and release efficiency were found to be ∼91 and ∼80 %, respectively, indicating that the Mn-, N-CQD has an excellent capability toward loading and releasing of MTX. The results of MTT and live/dead assays revealed promising results, signifies that the Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex impart greater cytotoxicity toward HeLa cells than the free MTX, which further substantiates that Mn-, N-CQD/MTX complex is likely to be more effective than free MTX when used in cancer therapies. On the contrary, L929 cells were largely unaffected when incubated with both the MTX-conjugated Mn-, N-CQD and the free drug. This selective cytotoxicity suggests that the MTX-conjugated Mn-, N-CQD complex (a nano drug) effectively recognizes and targets cancer cells, allowing for enhanced drug uptake and accumulation within the malignant tissue. These outcomes open new possibilities for the bio-medical applications of Mn-, N-CQD as a nanocarrier for drug delivery.

Harish Singh: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Dhruba Dhar: Investigation, Formal analysis. Soumen Das: Supervision, Resources. Mintu Halder: Review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M. H. thanks to IIT Kharagpur for financial supports. HS thanks DST-INSPIRE for his fellowship. We thank Prof. N. Sarkar of Chemistry and Prof. J. Chatterjee of SMST for allowing us to perform DLS experiment and MTT assay in their labs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115692.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
